title: Mac Apps Dying After macOS Golden Gate: How to Check
body:
In a significant move, Apple has announced that it will be phasing out support for a crucial feature that has allowed Intel-based apps to run seamlessly on its latest Macs. The technology, known as Rosetta 2, has been a vital component in ensuring a smooth transition for users who have upgraded to Apple silicon Macs. However, with the upcoming release of macOS 28, Rosetta 2 will no longer be supported, rendering many Intel-based apps obsolete. This change is expected to have far-reaching implications for Mac users, developers, and the broader tech ecosystem.

For those unfamiliar with Rosetta 2, it is a translation layer that enables Intel-based apps to run on Apple's proprietary silicon chips. When Apple first introduced its Apple silicon Macs, many users were concerned about the compatibility of their existing apps. Rosetta 2 helped alleviate these concerns by allowing Intel-based apps to run on the new hardware, albeit with some performance compromises. However, with the impending demise of Rosetta 2, users will need to assess which of their favorite apps will continue to function and which will be left behind. This transition is likely to be particularly challenging for users who rely on niche or older apps that may not have been updated to support Apple silicon.

The phase-out of Rosetta 2 is a deliberate move by Apple to push developers towards creating native apps that are optimized for its silicon chips. By doing so, Apple aims to improve the overall performance, security, and efficiency of its Macs. While this move may seem abrupt, it is a natural progression in Apple's efforts to transition its ecosystem to its proprietary hardware. The company has been encouraging developers to create universal apps that can run seamlessly across both Intel-based and Apple silicon Macs. As a result, many popular apps have already been updated to support Apple silicon, minimizing the impact of Rosetta 2's demise.

Despite these efforts, there will undoubtedly be some apps that will no longer function after the release of macOS 28. Users can expect to see a notification when they launch an app that is not compatible with the new operating system. To mitigate this issue, Apple is likely to provide tools and resources to help users identify which apps will be affected and offer guidance on alternative solutions. Developers, on the other hand, will need to reassess their app portfolios and prioritize updates to ensure continued compatibility. This may involve significant investments in redevelopment, testing, and quality assurance.

As the tech industry continues to evolve, such transitions are inevitable. The shift to Apple silicon is a strategic move by Apple to differentiate its products and create a more integrated ecosystem. While the phase-out of Rosetta 2 may cause some short-term disruption, it is a necessary step towards a more streamlined and efficient Mac experience. As users and developers adapt to this change, they can expect to see improved performance, enhanced security, and innovative features that take advantage of Apple's proprietary hardware.

Looking ahead, the demise of Rosetta 2 serves as a reminder of the importance of staying up-to-date with the latest technology trends and developments. As Apple continues to push the boundaries of innovation, users and developers must be prepared to adapt and evolve. While the transition may seem daunting, it is an opportunity for the tech community to come together and create a more robust, efficient, and user-friendly ecosystem. As the release of macOS 28 approaches, users can expect a more seamless and integrated Mac experience, with apps that are optimized to take full advantage of Apple's cutting-edge hardware.
